observations.recommendation: We recommendedandtheManagementagreedthatthe: 1 PCF Custodian implement a process of regular review and monitoring of petty cash expenditures to ensure compliance with Section 1.1.2 Petty Cash Fund (PCF) and with the internal control mechanisms for petty cash operations. 2 to ensure proper accountability and segregation of duties, the Accountable Officer (AO) for the Petty Cash Fund be responsible for the custody of the fund. 3 Accountable Officer ensure that petty cash fund expenditures do not exceed the amount authorized by the approved CAFOA. Any anticipated overages require prior approval and a revised CAFOA; disposition of the fund; 4 PCF Custodian replenish the petty cash fund when disbursements reach 75% of the authorized amount, or as needed per Section 1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001. 5 PCF Custodian and Municipal Accountant thoroughly review all Petty Cash Vouchers (PCVs) for completeness and appropriateness before issuance; and vouchers in accordance with the checklist provided in COA Circular No. 2012-001 and the Accounting Unit shall conduct a thorough review of the transactions to verify their completeness and accuracy; 6 Local Finance Committee re-evaluate and adjust the petty cash fund for the MEEDMO Market, Water Works, and STCAST Office based on historical data of average monthly petty cash expenses.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the following: The agency commits to fast-track the implementation of a direct deposit payroll system as a means of improving payroll processing and reducing associated risks. Cash advances for personnel payments will be used only in exceptional cases, as permitted by applicable rules and regulations.

We recommended and the Management agreed with the Municipal Accounting Office and General Services Office to: 8 conduct the physical inventory count without delay and subsequently prepare and submit the overdue Reports on the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI); 9 prioritize the evaluation and implementation of suitable inventory management system to address current limitations; 10 ensure staff are trained on how to generate and submit the required reports (SC, RSMI, RPCI) accurately and on time; 11 provide comprehensive training on staff on inventory management principles, system usage and report generation; 12 GSO and the Municipal Accounting Office work together that their system and records are reconciled and up to date.

We recommended and the Management agreed to continue exhausting all possible remedies to collect the dormant receivables. The actions include: Preparation and submission of the following to the Commission on Audit: 1. Schedule of Dormant Receivables/Unliquidated Advances, 2. Schedule of Other Dormant Accounts; To secure/collate all available documents relative to the said accounts; To coordinate with the officer/s or employee/s responsible for handling the account in case of absence of records and documents and recommend the conduct of an investigation if applicable; To facilitate the filing with/through the ATL and/or RSA/SA, depending on the jurisdictional amount, a request for the approval of COA to write-off/derecognize from the books the dormant accounts.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the following: 14 Discontinue the practice of granting large cash advances for procurement of welfare goods and logistics for relief operations. Direct the Local Finance Committee and the MDRRMO to prohibit the use of cash advances for suppliers through checks supported by proper documentation, to enhance transparency, accountability, and efficiency; Require the integration of proper procurement and payment measures in the Camp Management Plan and in the Standard Operating Procedures of disaster response to ensure compliance with procurement, accounting, and auditing regulations. 15 Immediately cease the practice of authorizing cash advances exceeding P50,000.00 for regular and planned festivals. Ensure posting on PhilGEPS and compliance with RA 9184 for procurements exceeding the threshold. 16 Implement comprehensive reforms by strictly enforcing PhilGEPS posting for all procurements exceeding P50,000.00, establishing transparent procurement timelines to eliminate excessive delays. 17 Submit the original supporting documents for all cash advance liquidations. Specifically, the accountable officer should be required to replace invoices, and other relevant documentation to avoid issuance of Notice of Disallowance.

We recommended and the Management agreed to take immediate action to: 18 Designate clear storage areas, implement FEFO/FIFO stock rotation, enforce safe handling and stacking, conduct regular quality inspections, provide comprehensive staff training. Establish or upgrade an inventory management system to support accurate recording, tracking, and timely replenishment; assign trained personnel responsible for overseeing warehouse operations. 19 Review the current distribution of staff, the actual workload of each position, and the required skills for each role: the goal is to reallocate personnel to receive adequate staffing. 20 Ensure that all staff involved in inventory management are trained on proper documentation procedures.

We recommended and Management agreed to the following: 21 require the concerned personnel to submit duly corrected DTRs with complete and authenticated entries, or an official certification from their department head to validate the submitted records and ensure compliance with documentation requirements and prevent potential audit disallowances; submit pertinent documentary requirements related to the payment of Health Emergency Allowance to the Commission on Audit. 22 directed the department head to thoroughly review and validate the submitted records to ensure compliance with documentation requirements and prevent potential audit disallowances.

We recommended and the Management agreed to develop a comprehensive disaster response plan that includes clear timelines for the distribution of financial assistance and to reflect best practices and lessons learned. The MDRRM Officer and Acting MSWDO commit to timely distribution of financial assistance and to reflect the LGU’s Disaster Response and Rehabilitation Plan in the Standard Operating Procedures; monitor beneficiary verification, disbursements, and reporting.

We recommended and the Management agreed to: 24 strict enforcement of the submission of complete and properly accomplished Daily Time Records (DTRs) as a prerequisite for payroll disbursements. Non-compliance to submit duly accomplished DTRs for CY2024 will result in issuance of Notice of Disallowance; submit the deficiencies as discussed in the audit observation. 25 the HRMO and Accounting Department implement a stricter verification process for payroll-related documents, ensuring that all disbursements are duly supported and properly computed. The concerned offices will implement review and document the deficiencies identified during payroll processing and establish timelines to support the standard workflow and timelines for the submission and validation of DTRs and payroll-related documents.

We recommended and the Management agreed to: 26 Immediately implement corrective action for the responsible parties, revise internal procedures for certification and payment, and enhance oversight to prevent recurrence. The Municipal Engineer will promptly notify the contractor to install the thermoplastic portion of the project, proceed with the appropriate inspections and monitoring, and ensure compliance with approved specifications and timeline. 27 Pursue the imposition of liquidated damages against the contractor for the delayed delivery of the “Part F. Miscellaneous Structure” line item, as stipulated in the contract.

We recommended and the Management agreed to renew/update issued PARs every three years after issuance or whenever there is a change in custodianship to establish proper accountability and to update the Inventory System to facilitate the renewal of issued PARs and the generation of updated property numbers.

We recommended and Management agreed to: 29 Immediately settle/liquidate cash advances on or before 2nd Quarter of CY2025 pursuant to COA-DBM-DILG-DND-DOF Joint Circular No. 2015-01 and COA Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015; the concerned Accountable Officers and Disbursing Officers commit to submit the liquidation report to the concerned Disbursing Officer. 30 Provide to the audit team the proof of transmittal of liquidation documents sent to ICFAU, COA, and the Quezon City Audit Team once the liquidation report and related supporting documents are submitted.

We recommended and the Management agreed to: 31 Ensure that these (3) projects undergo a thorough review and evaluation. All project phases should be covered within the original timeframe or revised to minimize the need for variation in costs and time; institute penalties and other forms of recourse provided by law for unsatisfactory performance. 32 undertake strict and adequate supervision and monitoring of the contractor of these (3) projects and impose liquidated damages, if warranted. These projects will be referred to the Project Monitoring Committee for quarterly monitoring and imposition of liquidated damages where applicable.

observations.managementAction: 2015 AAR | The management submitted only vouchers that are paid thru cash under the GF.
2015 AAR | The management committed to comply with the audit recommendation.
2015 AAR | The management did not conduct physical inventory or properties during the year.
2015 AAR | The Municipal Mayor instructed the officials concerned to comply with the audit recommendation.
2015 AAR | No Bank Reconciliation submitted to the Auditor.
2015 AAR | The LGU was unable to pay loan amortizations.
2015 AAR | The management committed to comply with the audit recommendation.
2015 AAR | The management promised to comply with the audit recommendation starting CY2016.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor observe due diligence in the exercise of its basic powers, duties and functions, specifically what is emphasized under Section 444,(b) (1)(x), Article One, Chapter 3, R.A. 7160 by directing the Municipal Accountant and the ICO-Municipal Treasurer, to collectively cause timely submission of the paid disbursement vouchers and payrolls including related reports of disbursements, used accountable forms and corresponding reports of collections and remittances, financial and other reports, and monthly/quarterly/yearly financial statements within the reglamentary period. Otherwise, appropriate charges shall be instituted against the officials who are primarily responsible for violating the requirements of law, rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to maintain General and Subsidiary Ledger and Treasury records, respectively, for all funds and present them to the Office of the Auditor for easy verification of account balances and to record all financial transactions in the corresponding books of accounts pursuant to the provisions of Volume 11, COA Circular No. 2002-003, dated June 20, 2002 to ensure the proper accounting of all financial transactions of the municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the following:
The Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary ledger and prepare the schedules to support the balances of the above trust liabilities;
The Municipal Accountant and the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to cause the prompt remittance to the BIR the taxes withheld from employees and suppliers/contractors to avoid accumulation of penalties and surcharges against the Municipality.
The Municipal Accountant to reconcile the employee’s master list with GSIS records. Cause the prompt remittance of the GSIS compulsory contributions of both employee’s and employer’s share as well as the loan repayments withheld in accordance with RA No. 8291 to avoid accumulation of two (2) percent penalties and other surcharges and likewise to avoid suspension of all loan privileges.
The Municipal Accountant and the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to remit the premium contributions and loan repayments to PAG-IBIG and PHILHEALTH to avoid imposition of penalties and surcharges and to avail of the corresponding benefits.
The OIC-Municipal Treasurer to remit the LGU shares due the Province and barangays in a regular/timely manner. Funds from this source could be utilized for the timely implementation of projects for the benefit of the barangays and the Province of Isabela as a whole.

We suggested that the Municipal Mayor should start the remittance of trust liabilities pertaining to the current year so as not to add to the already huge amount. Thereafter, reduce the outstanding obligation by accelerating the payment until the total obligation is fully paid. Coordinate with the concerned agencies for the condonation of penalties.

We recommended that management reconstitute the Inventory Committee to regularly conduct a complete physical inventory of the municipality’s property to establish the existence of the PPE. It is further required that timetable/deadlines set by management be strictly followed and that required reports be submitted pursuant to COA Circular No. 92-386. We likewise recommended for the Municipal Accountant to compute depreciation expense on depreciable assets every year pursuant to the Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standard (PPSAS) No. 17.

We recommended full and complete compliance with the requirements of the Full Disclosure Policy required under DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2011-134, to ensure transparency on its operations and transactions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instructs the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to apply the civil remedies provided for by law under Sections 174 (a) and (b) and Section 175 (a) of R.A. No. 7160 to enforce the collection of tax. Concerned officials should be reminded to be vigilant in the issuance or execution of levy within one (1) year from the time tax became delinquent.

Also, require the Municipal Assessor to update all information pertaining to taxable real property to have an accurate basis in the set-up of RPT/SET receivables.

Likewise, deputize Barangay Treasurers to collect taxes on real property located within the barangay to intensify tax collections.

We recommended that before payment is to be made on the transaction of the municipality, the supporting documents that are legal and proper should be attached to the disbursement vouchers. This is also to avoid suspension/disallowance in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to prepare regularly bank reconciliation statements and to submit to the audit team for verification.

We reiterated our recommendation for the timely submission of the monthly bank reconciliation statements together with the negotiated checks and the debit and credit memos issued by the bank to the audit team for verification.

We reiterate our recommendation for the Municipal Mayor to comply with the provisions of PD 1445 with regard to complete documentation of all their transactions so as to avoid further suspension/disallowance in audit. The granting of financial assistance of substantial amount is not in keeping with sound financial management considering that the cash position of the LGU cannot meet its current mandatory obligation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor follow the terms and conditions of the restructuring of the loan to avoid payment of accrued interests, penalties and surcharges and that the amount should be included in the succeeding budget until the loan is fully settled.

We recommended that the Municipal Planning Development Coordinator (MPDC) furnish timely the audit team the list of projects in detail under the Development fund and the status so as to conduct validation/inspection of projects implemented during the year.  Furthermore, accounts pertaining to the disbursements should also be submitted for audit within the prescribed period in order for the audit team to conduct a timely audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to deposit all their collections to the AGDB, in compliance with laws, rules and regulations.

We also recommended the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to stop using collections for the payment of various expenses of the LGU and to maintain petty cash fund for payment of petty cash operating expenses.

observations.managementAction: 2015 AAR | The management instructed the concerned officials not to process vouchers without complete documents.
2015 AAR | The LCE instructed the concerned officials to comply with the audit recommendation.
